coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots promote charge transfer of Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 heterojunction for efficient photodegradation of RhB in visible region
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Xing
Du, Wenxin
Gong, Xinchao
Nguyen, Tat Thang
Chen, Xueqi
Song, Zihui
Guo, Minghui
coredata.dc:description: Novel visible light-driven photocatalysts with high separation efficiency of the photo-induced charges urgently need to be developed. In this work, a Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 core-shell heterostructure photocatalyst and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were fabricated via the hydrothermal method. Establishing a close surfacial contact between the CQD particles and this type-II heterojunction yielded the new CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 optical catalytic material. Degradation of the Rhodamine B dye under visible-light irradiation was used to evaluate its catalytic performance. CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 exhibited superior and stable activity, which can be attributed to the enrichment of surface oxygen vacancies, more easily driven by visible light, the excellent optical and electronic properties and a lower charge recombination. Various experiments demonstrated that the type-II charge transfer process facilitates the separation of electron-hole pairs at the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 interface. CQDs can act as cocatalysts to further promote efficient charge separation and transfer in such structure. Moreover, this work presents a mechanism and major active species underlying the reaction on photocatalytic system.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots promote charge transfer of Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 heterojunction for efficient photodegradation of RhB in visible region

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel visible light-driven photocatalysts with high separation efficiency of the photo-induced charges urgently need to be developed. In this work, a Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 core-shell heterostructure photocatalyst and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were fabricated via the hydrothermal method. Establishing a close surfacial contact between the CQD particles and this type-II heterojunction yielded the new CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 optical catalytic material. Degradation of the Rhodamine B dye under visible-light irradiation was used to evaluate its catalytic performance. CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 exhibited superior and stable activity, which can be attributed to the enrichment of surface oxygen vacancies, more easily driven by visible light, the excellent optical and electronic properties and a lower charge recombination. Various experiments demonstrated that the type-II charge transfer process facilitates the separation of electron-hole pairs at the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 interface. CQDs can act as cocatalysts to further promote efficient charge separation and transfer in such structure. Moreover, this work presents a mechanism and major active species underlying the reaction on photocatalytic system.

ce:section.ce:para: The widespread use of colored dyes directly affects both the human health and the environment. Currently, the large amount of harmful chemical dyes, which are used in the textile, printing, plastics, and cosmetic production, are the major pollutants [1,2]. Therefore, an increasing research in the semiconductor-based photocatalysis field is of pivotal importance. Significant progress has been made in the development of ultraviolet-driven (ULD) catalysts. However, due to their low solar energy utilization, their photocatalytic performance is far from satisfactory [3]. Therefore, the development of a highly efficient visible light-driven (VLD) photocatalyst is key to effectively exploit the high-energy photogenerated charges, which are produced during the photocatalytic process. In recent years, a broad variety of bismuth (III)-based semiconductors with high photoactivity, physical, and chemical stability, such as Bi2WO6 [2,4], Bi2MoO6 [1], and BiVO4 [5,6], have been investigated. Among them, the results on Bi2MoO6, a typical VLD photocatalyst [1,7], show that it can remove many toxic contaminants. For this reason, it is currently used in several photocatalytic studies related to environmental protection. For instance, Li and Wang et al. [1] synthesized a flower-like Bi2MoO6 to achieve the photodegradation of Rhodamine B (RhB); Yang et al. [7] demonstrated the visible light-induced RhB degradation of the Bi2MoO6 plasma photocatalyst. Despite these successful results, the catalytic performance of Bi2MoO6 remains rather low, due to the rapid recombination of the electron-hole pairs that severely limits its application. Several strategies have been proposed to enhance its performance [8,9]. For instance, the construction of a heterostructure system based on different varieties of semiconductors effectively suppresses the charge recombination process and improves the charge transport performance. Encouragingly, various high-performance Bi2MoO6-based heterojunctions, such as BiOBr–Bi2MoO6, Bi/Bi2MoO6 [7], and CeO2/Bi2MoO6 [1], have been reported. The results show that they successfully improve the charge separation rate and the photocatalytic performance.

Studies show that the CeO2-doped Bi2MoO6 heterojunction exhibits an excellent catalytic performance [1], which is attributed to the VLD nature of the CeO2 photocatalyst [10]. Previous investigations performed by the authors of this paper show that the introduction of ZrO2 into CeO2 (Ce:Zr molar ratio = 7:3) via a hydrothermal method produces a Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 solid solution oxide that improves the performance of CeO2. This ceria-based VLD photocatalyst exhibits abundant surface oxygen vacancies and an improved thermal stability. Moreover, it has been proved to be very efficient in removing pollutants. Therefore, Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 is a promising cocatalyst candidate to optimize the activity of Bi2MoO6, which can be used in either the type-II or the Z-scheme heterojunction. In this work, two excellent VLD photocatalysts were selected to obtain a Ce0.7Zr0.3O2/Bi2MoO6 type-II heterostructure photocatalyst.

The literature shows that a carbon-based material graphene [11–13] is often used as a cocatalyst in the heterostructure photocatalyst, leading to the occurrence of photo-induced electron transfer [14], thus improving the performance of the heterojunction. However, this technique presents several disadvantages due to the harsh preparation conditions of graphene, the high cost of the raw materials, and the environmental damage that it may induce [15]. Thus, in this work, novel carbon nanomaterials, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [16], were investigated. They possess an approximately quasi-spherical structure with a diameter ˂10 nm, abundant edge defects [17], excellent optical properties, low toxicity, and outstanding electron-inducing ability to facilitates a rapid photoinduced charge transfer [16,18,19]. They are attractive for cocatalysts applied on photocatalytic systems [18]. Researches tried to synthesize CQDs from cheap carbon sources or directly from biomass materials, such as cellulose, lignin, egg whites, cellulose, and melons, over the years [20–23]. This study shows that the use of wood as the carbon source without the addition of any heavy metal results in low production costs as well as less pollution. The CQDs exhibit a “traditional” molecular structure and composition and their characteristics such as cocatalysts, match the ones reported in previous studies.

In this study, the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 and Bi2MoO6 photocatalysts, as well as the CQDs were respectively fabricated via the hydrothermal method. Then, the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 nanoparticles were used as the raw material and added into the hydrothermal synthesis of Bi2MoO6. In this fashion, the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 core@shell photocatalyst was obtained. Subsequently, a new photocatalytic system of CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 was synthesized by using CQDs as cocatalysts, whereas the RhB dye was used as the target pollutant, which was degraded by using visible light to evaluate the catalytic performance of the photocatalyst. The results show that after the adsorption of the CQDs, the photoactivity of Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 significantly improves. Various experimental methods have been used to study the photochemistry properties of these photocatalysts in detail.

This work used Larch wood as the raw material of CQDs. The reagents, including Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, Zr(NO3)4·5H2O, ammonia, ethyl alcohol, Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, and Rhodamine B, were purchased from Tianjin Fuchen chemical reagent factory (China) and used without further purification.
Carbon quantum dots were produced via a hydrothermal process, and the detailed preparation process has been added in Supporting Information.
Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 was produced via the hydrothermal process, and the detailed preparation process has been added in Supporting Information.

The morphology of the samples was observed via scanning electron microscopy (SEM, HITACHI S−520). The microstructures of the samples were determined via transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100). The elemental analysis of the samples was carried out by using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) on a Bruker Quantax 400. The functional groups and the molecular structures of the samples were analyzed via a Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR, EQUNOX-55, Germany BRUKER) in an operating frequency range of 500–4000 cm−1. The crystal structure of the specimens was obtained via an X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis using a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with an operating energy of 40 KV/100 mA. A series of Raman spectra was recorded by using an HR800 laser Raman spectrometer (JY, France) at the excitation wavelength of an argon ion laser (514 nm) and with a measured power of 20 mW. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed by using a PerkinElmer PHI-1600 ESCA spectrometer. The vacuum level was employed as the internal binding energy standard. Photoluminescence emission (PL) spectra and photoluminescence decay spectra were recorded by a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer with a 450 W xenon lamp in the 200–900 nm range. The UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra were obtained by using a U-3010Uve spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) in the 200–900 nm spectral range. Moreover, the diffuse reflectance spectra were calibrated with the BaSO4 standard. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded in the 200–800 nm range by using a TU-1901 ultraviolet–visible double-beam spectrophotometer. The photocurrent was measured via a three-electrode electro-chemical system (CHI-660E, China). A 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution was used as the electrolyte an Ag/AgCl electrode as a reference electrode. A Pt electrode was employed as an auxiliary electrode.

The visible light photocatalytic reaction device used in this work is almost identical to the ultraviolet light photocatalytic one with the only exception that a filter plate was added onto the 500 W Xe lamp (Beijing Trusttech Co. Ltd, China; PLS-SXE-300UV). In each experiment and during the stability tests, 0.05 g of the photocatalysts were dispersed into a RhB solution (10 mg L−1, 100 mL) and stirred for 5 min until they completely dispersed. The mixture was magnetically stirred for 30 min in a dark environment to reach an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The dispersion was kept under constant air-equilibrated conditions during the light irradiation experiments. A quantity of 2 mL of solution was collected from the initial mixture every 15 min for 90 min to calculate the RhB concentration. The system's absorbance was determined via ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry and the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded in the 200–800 nm range by using a TU-1901 ultraviolet–visible double-beam spectrophotometer. The degradation efficiency of RhB was calculated by using Eq. (1):
(1) Degradation (%) = [(C0 – C)/C0] × 100
where C0 and C represent the initial concentration of RhB after the adsorption-desorption equilibrium and the concentration of RhB at an interval of 15 min, respectively.

The chemical composition of the CQDs was analyzed via XPS. The high-resolution C 1s and O 1s spectra (Supplementary Figs. 1a and 1b) indicate the existence of various chemical bonds on the surface of the CQDs. The C 1s spectrum can be fitted with four peaks, which are attributed to the C–H/C–C/CC, C–O, CO, and –COOR bonds, whereas the O 1s peaks correspond to the O–CO/Ar–O−Ar, C–O/C–O–C, and Ar–OH bonds. This result demonstrates that the CQDs contain typical oxygen-containing functional groups. The C–O–C and Ar–O−Ar bonds maybe formed by dehydration of the hydroxyl groups in wood during the hydrothermal process. The –COOH, –OH, O–CO, and C–O bonds are responsible for the good water-solubility, which increase their stability in an aqueous solution [18]. The UV–vis spectra (Supplementary Fig. 1c) shows at 265.5 nm the typical absorption peak in UV region. This is due to the π→π* transition of the CC bond and n→π* transition of the CO bond, which are responsible for the optical properties [18–20]. The resulted CQDs are rich in “electron-withdrawing groups”, such as the carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups [24]. Thus, they possess an ability of photo-induction electron transfer [16]. From the perspective of electronic effect, electron transfer can occur between CQD molecule and photocatalyst, i.e. forming a D-A system, where CQDs are excellent electron acceptors. The photogenerated electrons in photocatalyst can be trapped efficiently by the electron-withdrawing groups in CQDs. The electron cloud is biased toward CQDs, which causes the electron cloud density of CQDs to rise. At this stage, the CQD molecules with a positive charge generate a positive electric field on their exterior, effectively trapping the photogenerated electrons, which are produced from the photocatalysts. This enhances the separation of electron-hole pairs. The Raman spectra of the CQDs is shown in Fig. S1d of Supporting Information. The peak at 1393.6 cm−1 is attributed to the D band, which belongs to the sp3 carbon atoms, and refers to the defects in the structure. The peak at 1590.7 cm−1 corresponds to the G band belonging to the ordered amorphous carbon structure, which contains the stretching vibration of sp2 carbon atoms. The C–H and C–C bonds are responsible for linking sp2/sp3 hybrid carbon atoms. The peak intensity ratio ID:IG (AD:AG) is often used to estimate the ratio of the sp3:sp2. The peak areas were quantified, and the estimated ID:IG ratio of the CQDs measures 0.37. This value is related to the degree of disorder and confirms the presence of the defect sites onto the surface of the CQDs [17,25]. These defects can act as trapping centers [16,18] to facilitate the photocatalytic performance. Further details about this process are provided in the following Sections. These results indicate that the chemical composition and the structure of the CQDs obtained in this work are in agreement with the carbon dots produced by using more traditional techniques.

A series of photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of the CQDs was obtained by monitoring different excitation wavelengths (Supplementary Fig. 2a). The results show that the emission wavelength gradually red-shifts within the 425–514 nm range upon increasing the excitation wavelength from 300 to 440 nm. This process is accompanied by a decrease in the peak intensity due to the difference in the surface chemical bonds and in the size of the CQD particles [26,27]. The coordinate positions of the CQDs for different excitation wavelengths in the CIE (Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage 1931 chromaticity) chromaticity diagram are displayed in Fig. S2b of Supporting Information. The data shows that the CQD aqueous solution emits bright blue and green colors. Similar optical properties have been previously reported for other carbon dots [17,18]. In summary, the CQDs fabricated by using wood as a raw material via the hydrothermal method exhibit a molecular structure, which is similar to the traditionally manufactured carbon dots and have excellent optical and cocatalytic properties.

Fig. 1 shows the SEM images of the photocatalysts. The Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 compound preliminarily exhibits the morphology of nanoparticles (Fig. 1a). The morphology of Bi2MoO6 displays a series of randomly oriented nanosheets (Fig. 1b). Fig. 1c shows that the morphology of Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 retains a nanoparticle structure. As shown in Fig. 2 , the morphological analysis further confirms the microstructure of Ce0.7Zr0.3O2, Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 and CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 via TEM and high resolution (HR)TEM images: the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 nanoparticle shows a cube morphology (Fig. 2a). By adding the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 nanoparticles into the synthesis process of Bi2MoO6, a core@shell heterostructure can be formed (Fig. 2b and c). A layer of Bi2MoO6 film covers the surfaces of the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 particles. The thickness of the shell and that of core are ~4.2 nm and ~10.4 nm (Fig. 2c). This implies that Bi2MoO6 may start the polymerization process by binding to Ce0.7Zr0.3O2. The HRTEM image of the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 heterojunction (Fig. 2d) shows that the particles with two types of lattice fringe directions exhibit an interplanar spacing of ~0.26 nm and ~0.37 nm. During this process the {100} and {111} facets, which correspond to the cubic CeO2 and orthorhombic Bi2MoO6 crystals [1,7,9,28], are mostly exposed. The HRTEM image of the CQDs (Fig. 2e) reveals that the particles are almost spherical and have a particle size <10 nm. These observations are in good agreement with the basic morphologies of the traditional carbon dots. In addition, the HRTEM image of CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 is analyzed (Fig. 2f): several particles are present onto the surface of the heterojunction, as the TEM image of a single CQD suggests (arrow). This confirms that the CQDs load onto the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 surface, i.e. the surface of Bi2MoO6 shell. Fig. 2h shows the corresponding EDS spectrum of the CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 compound in Fig. 2g. The detected Cu elements are mostly originated by the conducting resin and the metal spraying. Moreover, the Bi, Mo, Ce, Zr, O, and C elements are observed. These results confirm that the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst with a core@shell heterostructure was synthesized. In addition, a strong surfacial contact between the CQDs and Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 was achieved and the CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 photocatalytic system was obtained.

The XRD patterns record the crystalline phases of the photocatalysts (Fig. 3 a). The diffraction peaks of CeO2 can be assigned to the normal XRD pattern of cubic CeO2 phase (JCPDS 34–0394) [29]. The Ce0.7Zr0.3O2, diffraction peaks that appear can be assigned to the resulted CeO2 phase. There is no presence of other peaks, indicating the high dispersion of a small quantity of ZrO2 in the CeO2 crystal [30]. Due to the lack of normal XRD pattern of the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 compound, the existing standard XRD pattern of CeO2 was used to consider the measured pattern of Ce0.7Zr0.3O2, which are consistent with cubic structure. The Bi2MoO6, all of the diffraction peaks can be well-indexed to the normal XRD pattern of the orthorhombic Bi2MoO6 phase (JCPDS 77–1246) [1,7,9]. The diffraction peaks of the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 and CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 photocatalysts can be indexed to the orthorhombic Bi2MoO6 phase. The Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 phase cannot be detected and this is attributed to the low content of Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 in the sample. In addition, the diffraction peak intensity of Bi2MoO6 in the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 and CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 appears to be weaker than in the bare Bi2MoO6 phase. The reason for this phenomenon may lay in the decrease of the Bi2MoO6 crystallinity due to the formation of the heterojunction. However, no characteristic peak belonging to the CQDs was found in the XRD pattern of the CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 sample. When these results are compared to the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 specimen, it appears that each phase in the CQDs-loaded Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 sample retains its original crystal structure, demonstrating that the CQDs are highly dispersed onto the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 surface.

To further investigate the internal vibrations of the chemical bonds of the samples, the Raman spectra of the photocatalysts were recorded (Fig. 3b). The Bi2MoO6 exhibits a peak, which is located at 388.2 cm−1 and belong to the stretching and the deformation vibration modes of Bi–O. The peak at 447.0 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of Mo–O–Bi [31]. The features at 815.6, 898.1, and 948.3 cm−1 represent the stretching vibration of the Mo–O bonds in the MoO6 2− octahedron [7,9]. The Raman vibration peak at 435.6 cm−1 in the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 spectrum belongs to the Ce–O stretch, whereas the weak vibration at 582.5 cm−1 can be attributed to the characteristic vibration of the surface defects [29,30]. The Raman vibration peaks, which can be observed in the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 and in the CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst, belong to the Bi2MoO6 without the vibration peaks of Ce0.7Zr0.3O2, since Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 is wrapped by the Bi2MoO6 structure. By comparing these observations to pure Bi2MoO6, the results show that the peaks are significantly shifted toward lower wavenumbers. The internal vibration mode and the wavenumber exhibit the following relation [32]:
(2)
v ‾
=
1
2 π c
k μ
where ν̅, c, k, and μ represent the wavenumber, the velocity of an electromagnetic wave, the force constant of the chemical bond, and the reduced mass of the two atoms, respectively. According to Eq. (2), the decrease in the Raman wavenumber may correspond to a decrease in the force constant. This implies an increase in the bond length and a reduction in the bond energy [32]. According to the relation between the bond energy of the chemical bond and the electron excitation energy [33], the lower the bond energy is, the lower is the energy required to excite the electrons: the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 and CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 photocatalysts can then more easily be driven by photons. Two intense peaks (red arrow) are observed in both the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 and the CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 samples. They are generated by the split of the 948.3 cm−1 Raman peak of Bi2MoO6. This indicates that the two double resonance scattering process occurs in the sample which is triggered by a Fermi Resonance phenomenon within the molecules [33]. The coupling of the molecular vibrations induces the generation of a coupling frequency, which deviates from the fundamental frequency: since two frequency shifts-one toward high frequencies and one toward low frequencies - take place, the Raman peak splits.

The FT-IR spectra help analyzing the chemical bonds present onto the photocatalysts (Fig. 3c). The Bi2MoO6 peak at 840.6 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the Mo–O bonds in the MoO6 2− octahedron, whereas the peak at 710.9 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of the Mo–O–Bi bonds. The peak at 566.4 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the Bi–O bonds [7,34]. Similarly, the same regions of FT-IR spectra were observed for the Bi2MoO6, Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6, and the CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 samples. The peaks in the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 and CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 photocatalysts correspond to the stretching vibrations of the Mo–O–Bi bonds, which are located at 718.8 cm−1 at a slightly higher wavenumber. These results demonstrate that when heterojunction atoms combine into molecular crystals, different atoms compete for valence electrons, i.e. the electron cloud density distribution changes, resulting in an inductive effect. After the formation of heterojunction crystal, the whole system tends to be stable, i.e. the bond energy and force constant of the chemical bond decrease, and results in a high shift of the group frequency [35,36].

The surface element composition of the photocatalysts was characterized via XPS. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the XPS spectra of Ce 3d in Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 shows eight binding energy peaks, which can be attributed to the Ce 3d3/2 spin-orbit states (916.5, 908, 900.5, 898, 888.7, and 882.3 eV) and to the Ce 3d5/2 states (902.7 and 884.9 eV), indicating that the Ce ions exist in the +3 and + 4 states [37–39]. However, no XPS peak of Ce 3d was detected for the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 and CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 photocatalysts. This confirms that these peaks are quenched due to the wrapping structure of Bi2MoO6. A similar effect in the Zr 3d XPS data was observed (Fig. 4b): the spectrum shows two binding energy peaks, which can be attributed to the Zr 3d3/2 bond (181.9 eV) and the Zr–O bond (184.4 eV) and were quenched in the heterostructure photocatalytic systems [40].

The Bi 4f and Mo 3d spectra of Bi2MoO6, Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6, and CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 show two binding energy peaks (Fig. 4c and d) in the 163.9–164.3 eV and 158.7–159.1 eV regions. They correspond to the Bi 4f5/2 and the Bi 4f7/2 bonds, respectively [1,7,9]; and the features in the 235–235.2 eV and 231.8–232.1 eV regions correspond to the Mo 3d3/2 and the Mo 3d5/2 bonds, respectively [1,7,9] and they indicate the presence of Bi3+ and Mo6+ in these samples. When compared to the pure Bi2MoO6 case, the binding energies of Bi 4f and Mo 3d shift toward lower and higher energy, respectively in the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 and in the CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 photocatalysts. This implies that: 1) weak chemical bonds combined with the Bi atoms are formed in both heterostructure photocatalytic systems, in agreement with the Raman results; 2) the shift of Mo 3d may cause an increase in the shielding effect on Bi 4f, due to the smaller electron cloud surrounding Mo6+. This increases the electron density of Bi 4f in the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 and CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 photocatalysts [1,40], which is in agreement with the FT-IR results.

The surface oxygen vacancies, i.e. the central sites of the catalytic activity, play a critical role in the photocatalytic reaction [39]. As shown in Fig. 4e, the asymmetric O 1s spectra of the samples can be fitted by two components both in the low binding energy region (529.4–530.5 eV) and in the higher one (531.3–531.8 eV and 532.9–533.1 eV). They belong to the lattice oxygen (O2−), which is labeled as Oβ, and to the chemisorbed active oxygen (O2 −, O2 2−, or O−) species on the oxygen vacancy sites, which is labeled as Oα. The corresponding fitted peak areas of O 1s curve and their peak area ratios of Oα to Ototal are listed in Table 1 . The relative peak area ratios of Oα/Ototal, which correspond to the relative content ratios of surface oxygen vacancies [41,42], changes in the compounds as follows: Bi2MoO6 (45%) < Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 (53%) < Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 (63%) < CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 (84%). Adsorption of the CQDs onto the surfaces produce a higher number of oxygen vacancy sites, which act as catalytic active sites during the photocatalysis process. Thus, more active species are attracted to the sites, which contributes to the enhancement of their photocatalytic performance.

The UV–visible absorption spectra of the photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 5 and also calculated their optical band gap energies. The absorption band edges of the photocatalysts lie in the >440 nm range. This implies that they may be responsible for the visible-light induced photocatalytic activity. Generally, a higher light absorption property constitutes one of the main reasons to explain the excellent performance of the photocatalysts [1]. Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 and Bi2MoO6 exhibit distinct absorption from 200 nm–504 nm and 200 nm–465 nm, respectively. The formation of heterojunction apparently obtains an improved absorption property (200 nm–607 nm). After adsorbing CQDs, the absorption band edge significantly extends to 848 nm. This explains the intense light absorption property of such photocatalytic system. The formula for calculating the optical band gap energy can be expressed as:
(3) Eg (eV) = 1240/λg

λg (nm) is the absorption wavelength threshold value. The results show that the CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 photocatalytic system shows the minimum band gap energy among the investigated photocatalysts: when Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 (Eg ~ 2.46 eV) and Bi2MoO6 (Eg ~ 2.67 eV) form the heterojunction, the lower band gap in the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 compound can be obtained (Eg ~ 2.04 eV); after the adsorption of the CQDs onto its surface, the band gap further lowers (Eg ~ 1.46 eV) and tends to absorb larger portions of light.

The photogenerated electron lifetimes of the photocatalysts were analyzed via time-resolved photoluminescence (PL). The spectra are reported in Fig. 6 a. The model, which was used to calculate the PL lifetimes can be written as follows:
(4)

The fitting equation of time-resolved fluorescence spectra is E-exponential model:
(5)

( n = 1 , 2 , 3...... )
where A1, A2, τ1 and τ2 are the resultant parameters obtained by fitting. The relation between the PL lifetime and the charge transfer process in a photocatalytic material involves two aspects [43–47]: 1) if the effective charge transfer is an unidirectional process, which starts from the photocatalyst and ends to the cocatalyst, a shortened PL lifetime can be obtained; 2) if the effective charge transfer is a bidirectional process, such as in a type-II heterostructure, a prolonged PL lifetime is achieved [43]. The PL lifetimes of the samples can be calculated by using Eq. (4). The results can be summarized as follows: 1) The average PL lifetime in the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 heterojunction is longer (5.166 μs) than for Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 (4.948 μs) and Bi2MoO6 (4.989 μs). This demonstrates the existence of a type-II charge transfer process; 2) After the adsorption of the CQDs, the average PL lifetime in the CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 photocatalytic system is shortened to 4.909 μs? This indicates that the CQDs act as cocatalysts, play a positive role in trapping the photogenerated electrons. This process effectively promotes the charge transfer efficiency in the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 heterojunction.

The band structure of the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 heterojunction was analyzed to obtain further details about the type-II charge transfer process [1]. The conduction band (CB) potential can be calculated via Eq. (6):
(6) ECB = χ - Ec - 0.5Eg
where ECB corresponds to the conduction band energy, χ is the absolute electronegativity of the semiconductor (for Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 the calculated value is 5.63 eV; for Bi2MoO6, according to the literature, it is 5.5 eV [34]), Ec represents the energy of the free electrons on the hydrogen scale (~4.5 eV). Therefore, based on the results reported on the band gaps, the calculated conduction band values of Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 and Bi2MoO6 measure −0.10 eV and −0.34 eV, respectively. The valence band (VB) potential can be calculated by using Eq. (7):
(7) EVB = ECB + Eg
where EVB corresponds to the valence band energy. The calculated valence bands of Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 and Bi2MoO6 measure 2.36 eV and 2.34 eV, respectively. During the photocatalytic process, when the heterojunction photocatalyst is exposed to light with an energy higher than the band gap energy, the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 and Bi2MoO6 electrons are stimulated from the valence band and transferred into the conduction band, respectively. In this fashion, electron-hole pairs are generated. Based on the calculated CB:VB edge potentials, the CB potential of Bi2MoO6 (−0.34 eV) is more negative than that of Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 (−0.10 eV). For this reason, the electrons, which are photogenerated from the CB of Bi2MoO6, can more easily transfer to the CB of Ce0.7Zr0.3O2, whereas the holes located on the VB of Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 can transfer to the VB of Bi2MoO6: this process is at the basis of the type-II electron transfer via the heterointerface [1].

The detection of the photoluminescence (PL) spectra was employed to investigate the photophysical properties of the samples (Fig. 6b). Under the excitation wavelength of 320 nm, the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 and CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 exhibit similar typical blue bands, mainly at 393.5 nm, which correspond to the 4f-5d transition of the Ce3+ ion. The result was obtained by comparing with Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6: the decrease in the PL emission intensity of the CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 system demonstrate a faster separation of electron/hole pair, since the CQDs promote the charge separation and type-II transfer, thus decreasing the recombination, accompaning by an energy transfer due to fluorescence quenching effect [48]. This nonradiative transition processes in CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 is also related to the enrichment of surface defect sites, which serve as fluorescence quenching sites and traps [48]. In this work, the partial energy inside the CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6, transferring to the surface defect sites, thus leading to the quenching of fluorescence.

Photocurrent response measurements are generally used to effectively assess the ability of the photogenerated charges to separate: a strong photocurrent response intensity corresponds to a high charge separation performance [49]. It can be observed from Fig. 7 , when CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 is compared to the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6, its photocurrent intensity enhances upon the adsorption of the CQDs on its surface: since the CQDs effectively trap the photogenerated electrons, the transport of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs is facilitated and this decreases the time response of the input light.

The catalytic performance of the photocatalysts was analyzed via the degradation of RhB. A blank experiment was carried out without adding the photocatalysts as a comparison. The results show that almost no self-degradation of RhB occurs before 90 min from the beginning of the test. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the dye-adsorption rates of the photocatalysts are all less than 15% during continuously stirring for 30 min (−30min ~ 0min) in the dark. After turning on the light source, the photodegradation efficiency of RhB on the photocatalysts are apparent. The UV light-driven photocatalytic activity of the photocatalysts is illustrated in Supplementary material. In visible region, when Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 and Bi2MoO6 form a heterojunction, the photocatalytic activity of the Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 one has some improvement. It is worth noticing that the CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 photocatalytic system exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity when compared to Ce0.7Zr0.3O2, Bi2MoO6 and Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6. At 45 min, the photodegradation percentage of RhB reaches 100% and this can be attributed to the strengthened charge transfer process. This result demonstrates that the electron trapping of the CQDs is key factors to enhance the photocatalytic activity. By using the pseudo-first-order model [1], the catalytic activity of a photocatalyst can be written as shown in Eq. (8):
(8) - ln(C/C0) = kt
where k corresponds to the apparent rate constant [1,34] and the kinetic rate of RhB degradation is measured. Fig. 8b shows that the k value of the CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst (0.09483 min−1) is 4.6, 4.2, and 1.6 times higher than that of Bi2MoO6 (0.02069 min−1), Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 (0.02269 min−1), and Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 (0.05935 min−1), respectively, due to its high photocatalytic activity. In this work, the CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 photocatalytic system is one of the most effective VLD photocatalysts for the RhB dye removal. The cycle test of the photocatalytic activity was performed to evaluate the stability of the CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6. The results are shown in Fig. 8c; After six cycle tests, the RhB degradation efficiency value remains stable, which can confirm its good stability and durability, can be observed.

In order to determine the active species present on the CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst that can effectively degrade the RhB pollutant, the K2S2O8, ammonium oxalate (AO), and benzoquinone (BQ), which are the quenchers of e–, h+ and •O2 −, respectively, were added into the mixture. As shown in Fig. 8d, the results show that upon the addition of AO, K2S2O8 and BQ, the efficiency of RhB decreases, indicating that: 1) h+ and •O2 − are active species in the CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst, which effectively degrade RhB; 2) under illumination, both Ce0.7Zr0.3O2 and Bi2MoO6 in the heterojunction can be easily driven by visible light photons, the electrons are excited, and the transition to the CB takes place, while h+ remains in the VB. CQDs effectively trap electrons from the CB, which promotes type-II charge transfer process. The trapped-electrons enrich onto the interface between photocatalyst and RhB solution, are able to effectively reduce the adsorbed O2 into superoxide ion radical (•O2 −). In such photocatalytic system, the separated electrons and holes participate in a series of reactions at the same time to maintain the charge transfer process, achieving superior photocatalytic performance.

In summary, CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 demonstrated an ameliorative photocatalytic performance under visible light irradiation. The most likely working mechanism is that the cocatalyst CQDs promoted the transfer charge of the photocatalysts. According to the experimental results, CQDs with electron-withdrawing groups possess the performance in photo-induced electron transfer, which can trap the photogenerated electrons produced from the photocatalysts when irradiated with visible light. The electron cloud is biased toward CQDs, which causes the electron cloud density of CQDs to rise. At this stage, the CQD molecule with a positive charge generates a positive electric field on its exterior, which promotes the charge transfer of the photocatalysts. The photogenerated electrons and holes that transferred onto the interface between dye and photocatalyst play a key role in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB. Based on the above discussion, the schematic diagram of photocatalytic mechanism over CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 is shown in Fig. 9 .

A series of VLD photocatalysts consisting a Ce0.7Zr0.3O2, Bi2MoO6 and Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 core-shell heterojunction, as well as CQDs were prepared via the hydrothermal method. CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 shows high and stable photocatalytic activity for RhB degradation upon visible light irradiation. Such results confirm that this system can be more easily driven by visible photons and has abundance of oxygen vacancies, a higher light absorption and charge transfer efficiency. CQDs act as cocatalysts and trap photogenerated electrons. This promotes the type-II charge transfer process in Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6. A photocatalytic mechanism was proposed: the numerous h+ and e– enriched on the surface of CQDs/Ce0.7Zr0.3O2@Bi2MoO6 participate in the reaction at the same time. Both h+ and •O2 − species play a pivotal role in the effective degradation of RhB. This improves and maintains the charge separation and transfer in the system.
